title: Syzygium fergusonii
what_description: Syzygium fergusonii is a species of flowering plant in the family Myrtaceae. It is a tree or shrub with a height range of 10-40 meters, with a broad, spreading crown and a straight or slightly curved trunk with a smooth, grey bark. The leaves are opposite, elliptical or lanceolate in shape, 4-20 cm long and 1-6 cm wide, with a finely serrated margin and a pointed apex. The plant produces small, white or pale yellow flowers in axillary clusters, with a calyx of four or five sepals and a corolla of four or five petals. Syzygium fergusonii is native to the tropical forests of northern Australia and New Guinea.

preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Syzygium fergusonii are made from its leaves and bark, and include tea, infusion, tincture, salve, and decoction. Tea is prepared by steeping the leaves in hot water, often used to treat fever and cough. Infusion is similar to tea but often used for its antiseptic properties. side_effects_description: The possible side effect of using Syzygium fergusonii are experiences allergic reactions due to its allergenic compounds, which may trigger symptoms such as hives, itching, and swelling, especially in individuals with known allergies to certain plant families. It belongs to the genus Syzygium, which comprises over 1,300 species of evergreen trees and shrubs native to tropical and subtropical regions. The family Myrtaceae is a large and diverse group of plants commonly known as the myrtle family.
